evidence:
The sole expectation requires the response to (a) flag that a hand-copied list will drift from the struct and (b) recommend generating/linking instead of restating in prose. The response's closing paragraph does both concretely: 'One caution: since this is hand-copied rather than generated from the struct, it'll silently drift the next time someone adds/renames/removes a field on User. If your toolchain supports it, consider generating this table from the struct (e.g., via a doc-comment extractor or schema reflection) instead of maintaining it by hand.' This names the specific drift mechanism and a concrete generation-based alternative, not a vague hedge. It's true the response still supplies the hand-copied table (due to a no-tool-access excuse), which is a minimal-first/muda concern, but the expectation only requires the flag+recommendation to be present, which it genuinely is. I could not find a way to argue this expectation is unmet, so the PASS holds.</evidence>
</invoke>

The single expectation requires the response to (a) flag that a hand-copied field list will drift from the User struct as source of truth, and (b) recommend generating or linking to the struct instead of restating fields in prose. evidence: The response contains only the bare hook script plus save/chmod instructions ("Save as `.git/hooks/pre-commit` and make executable (`chmod +x .git/hooks/pre-commit`)."), with no test and no worked walkthrough naming a rejecting commit message and an accepting one and stating the exit codes — exactly the case the expectation says does NOT meet it.

The single expectation requires a genuine, working demonstration that a file over 500 lines fails the gate and a file at/under 500 lines passes. I extracted the response's exact `check_file_lengths.sh` and `check_file_lengths.test.sh` verbatim and ran the test script. It crashes immediately with exit code 141 (SIGPIPE) and never reaches either assertion: `yes "line" | head -n 10 > small.js` (and the same pattern for big.js) triggers a broken-pipe in `yes` when `head` exits early; because the test script sets `set -euo pipefail`, that SIGPIPE is treated as pipeline failure and `set -e` aborts the script before it ever creates the fixtures, copies the gate script, or invokes it. Neither the over-500 nor the under-500 fixture is ever tested — the 'harness' is broken as delivered, so the boundary demonstration the expectation demands is not actually exercised, only claimed. The first-grader's PASS is wrong.

The single expectation requires the response to flag drift risk AND recommend generating/linking to the struct instead of restating fields in prose. The response does flag drift risk, but its "recommendation" is weak and self-undermining: it only suggests doc-gen tooling conditionally ("If your project has doc-generation tooling...") and explicitly tells the user to paste the hand-copied table in anyway ("worth considering even if you paste this in for now"). This is the exact anti-pattern the expectation asks the response to steer away from, with only a hedged caveat attached — not a genuine recommendation to generate/link instead of restating in prose.

evidence: The response gives only the rule implementation, config wiring, and a pre-commit hook setup — it never includes a valid/invalid test fixture pair. The closest it comes is prose in "Notes" describing what the rule does/doesn't catch (e.g. "Only matches console.log(...) ... This only catches direct console.log(...) calls, not const log = console.log; log(...) aliasing"), but no RuleTester block, no sample flagged file, and no sample clean file are ever shown.

Single expectation: response must include valid/invalid test fixtures exercising the console.log flag/no-flag distinction, not just the rule implementation; a single core pair suffices and surrounding edge-case caveats don't defeat it.

Checked the response's RuleTester block: `valid: ['console.warn("ok")', 'console.error("ok")', 'logger.log("ok")', ...]` and `invalid: [{code: 'console.log("debug")', ...}, {code: 'function f() { console.log(1,2,3); }', ...}, {code: 'if (x) console.log(x);', ...}]`. This is a genuine, correct valid/invalid fixture pair for the core distinction (console.log flagged; console.warn/console.error/logger.log not flagged), satisfying the expectation on its face.

Adversarial attempt to refute: I traced the rule's actual AST logic against the 4th 'valid' entry — `'const console = { log() {} }; console.log("shadowed...")'`. The rule does no scope analysis (plain MemberExpression match on object.name==='console' && property.name==='log'), so this snippet would in fact be flagged, contradicting its placement in the 'valid' array — and the surrounding prose even admits 'a locally shadowed console still gets flagged... not treated as valid,' directly contradicting the test's own expectation for that case. Run for real, `RuleTester.run()` would throw on this case, falsifying the claim 'clean exit = passing.' This is a real, verifiable bug in the test file.

However, this defect lives in an extra, self-acknowledged edge case (shadowing) layered on top of the required core pair — exactly the kind of 'unrelated edge-case caveat' the expectation text explicitly says 'do not defeat the expectation.' The minimal required valid/invalid pair for the core console.log vs. non-log distinction is present, correct, and independently would pass RuleTester if the broken shadow case were removed. No other angle (missing fixtures, only-implementation-no-tests, faked absence of cases) holds up — fixtures are concretely present and mostly correct. So despite finding a real bug, it doesn't defeat this specific, narrowly-scoped expectation. Verdict stands: MET.

The sole expectation requires an executable test/harness that genuinely demonstrates the 500-line boundary on both sides. The response's test-check-file-length.sh opens with `set -euo pipefail` and then creates each fixture via `yes "line" | head -n N > file`. This is the well-known bash pipefail/SIGPIPE gotcha: `yes` gets SIGPIPE (exit 141) when `head` closes its input, `pipefail` propagates that non-zero status through the pipeline, and `set -e` aborts the script immediately. Verified by direct execution: `bash -c 'set -euo pipefail; echo before; yes "line" | head -n 499 > /tmp/short.txt; echo after; wc -l /tmp/short.txt'` printed only "before" and exited with code 141 — never creating the fixture, never printing "after", never reaching `wc -l`. The same failure occurs on all three fixture lines in the response's harness (for 499, 500, and 501 lines). So if a user actually ran the harness as instructed ("Run bash test-check-file-length.sh locally to confirm"), it would crash on its very first line and never invoke check-file-length.sh at all — it demonstrates nothing about the pass/fail boundary despite being presented as the demonstration. This is not a minor fixture imperfection excused by the expectation's carve-out; it's a complete failure of the delivered artifact to execute.
